status.state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
status.product_description: Interactive coding bootcamp platform with browser-based IDE, auto-grading, code review bot, project-based curriculum, and job placement tracking.

plan.description: Define business requirements, user personas (students, instructors, employers), and core process flows for the coding bootcamp platform. Map out enrollment funnel, learning journey, job placement pipeline, and ROI model.
acceptance_criteria: At least 30 user stories written in Given/When/Then format covering all 4 personas
End-to-end process flow diagrams for enrollment, learning, and job placement exist
ROI model includes student acquisition cost, completion rate assumptions, and placement revenue projections
Stakeholder map approved by product lead

plan.description: Analyze the coding bootcamp market: competitor platforms (Codecademy, LeetCode, Replit Teams), differentiation opportunities, pricing benchmarks, and employer partnership models.
acceptance_criteria: Competitor matrix covers at least 5 platforms across 8 dimensions
Pricing benchmark table produced with free/paid tier analysis
At least 3 differentiation opportunities identified and scored by feasibility
Employer partnership model recommendations documented

plan.description: Conduct user research (interviews and surveys) with prospective students, instructors, and hiring managers. Produce personas, empathy maps, and key JTBD (Jobs-To-Be-Done) for each role.
acceptance_criteria: At least 3 validated personas with photo, goals, pain points, and tech comfort level
Empathy maps completed for student and instructor personas
JTBD statements defined for all core platform interactions
WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility requirements captured as UX constraints
Accessibility for learners with disabilities
Mobile-responsive for student devices

plan.description: Write the full Product Requirements Document (PRD), define OKRs, create a phased roadmap (MVP → v1 → v2), and establish success metrics for browser IDE, auto-grading, code review bot, curriculum, and job placement modules.
acceptance_criteria: PRD covers all 5 core modules with functional and non-functional requirements
Each phase has clearly defined MVP scope and exit criteria
Success metrics are measurable with baseline and target values defined
Roadmap reviewed and signed off by stakeholders

plan.description: Produce compliance plan and evidence artifacts for COPPA (users under 13), FERPA (student educational records), and WCAG 2.1 AA. Define data handling policies, parental consent flows, and accessibility audit schedule.
acceptance_criteria: Data flow diagram maps every PII/student-record touch point with storage location and retention period
COPPA age-gate and parental consent flow documented and legally reviewed
FERPA data inventory lists all student records, access controls, and disclosure rules
WCAG 2.1 AA checklist maps every UI module to specific success criteria
Privacy Impact Assessment signed off by legal_advisor

plan.description: Draft Terms of Service, Privacy Policy, Student Data Agreement, and Employer Partner Agreement. Ensure COPPA, FERPA, and GDPR clauses are included. Review IP ownership for student-submitted projects.
acceptance_criteria: All 4 legal documents drafted and reviewed by legal_advisor
Privacy Policy is GDPR/COPPA/FERPA compliant with clear data subject rights section
Student IP ownership clause explicitly grants students ownership of project code they write
Employer agreement defines permissible use of student data for recruitment
FERPA/COPPA compliance if minors involved

plan.description: Design wireframes and high-fidelity mockups for all core screens: student dashboard, browser IDE, course curriculum viewer, auto-grading results, code review feedback UI, job placement tracker, instructor dashboard, and employer portal.
acceptance_criteria: Wireframes cover all 12 screens with annotated interaction specs
Design system defines typography, color tokens, component library (buttons, inputs, code panels)
Browser IDE mockup includes file tree, code editor, terminal, and output panel
All designs pass automated WCAG 2.1 AA contrast and focus-order checks
Usability test conducted with 5 participants; critical issues resolved before handoff
Accessibility for learners with disabilities
Mobile-responsive for student devices

plan.description: Design the full database schema covering users, courses, lessons, projects, submissions, grades, code reviews, job listings, placement records, and audit logs. Produce ERD, migration scripts, and seed data.
acceptance_criteria: ERD reviewed and approved covering all entities with FK relationships
All PII fields annotated with FERPA/COPPA classification in schema comments
Alembic migrations run successfully on clean PostgreSQL instance
Seed data includes 2 sample courses, 10 lessons, 3 projects, and 5 demo users
Indexes defined for all foreign keys and high-frequency query columns

plan.description: Design the OpenAPI specification for all backend services: auth, user management, curriculum CRUD, submission pipeline, grading webhook, code-review bot endpoints, job listings, placement tracking, and instructor/employer APIs.
acceptance_criteria: OpenAPI spec validates without errors using swagger-cli
All endpoints have request/response schemas with example values
Auth scheme documented with role-based access (student, instructor, employer, admin)
Grading webhook endpoint specifies callback contract for async auto-grading results
Code review bot endpoints support streaming responses via SSE

plan.description: Bootstrap the monorepo structure: initialize frontend (React + TypeScript + Vite), backend (FastAPI + Python), and shared config. Set up environment variable management, Docker Compose for local dev, and pre-commit hooks.
acceptance_criteria: `make dev` starts all services (backend :8000, frontend :5173, postgres :5432) in under 60s
Pre-commit hooks enforce lint + format on every commit
`.env.example` documents every required environment variable with descriptions
OpenAPI client is auto-generated from openapi.yaml and committed to frontend/src/api/

plan.description: Design and implement cloud infrastructure: Kubernetes cluster (EKS/GKE), PostgreSQL RDS, Redis for session/queue, S3 for submission artifacts, CDN for static assets, and VPC network segmentation. Produce Terraform modules.
acceptance_criteria: Terraform plan completes without errors for all 3 environments
VPC has public/private subnet separation; backend and DB in private subnets only
RDS has automated backups enabled with 7-day retention
WAF rules block OWASP Top 10 attack patterns
S3 bucket has server-side encryption and public access blocked

plan.description: Produce threat model, security architecture review, and penetration test plan. Cover auth flows, IDE sandbox isolation, student data access controls, and code execution sandbox security.
acceptance_criteria: STRIDE threat model covers all 5 focus areas with mitigations mapped
Code execution sandbox isolation verified: student code cannot access host filesystem or network
RBAC matrix confirms students cannot access other students' submission data
SBOM generated listing all direct and transitive dependencies with CVE scan results
Pentest plan approved with scope, methodology, and remediation SLA defined

plan.description: Implement core backend services: authentication (JWT + OAuth2 with Google/GitHub), user management with RBAC, course/lesson CRUD, and the foundational project-submission pipeline.
acceptance_criteria: JWT tokens expire in 15 minutes with refresh token rotation
OAuth2 login works for both Google and GitHub providers
RBAC enforced: instructors can only CRUD their own courses; students cannot modify grades
All endpoints emit structured audit log events to audit_logs table
Course and lesson CRUD passes API contract tests against openapi.yaml

plan.description: Build the browser-based IDE backend: WebSocket session management, containerized code execution sandbox (Docker per-session), file system virtualization, language runtime support (Python, JS, Rust, Go), and output streaming.
acceptance_criteria: New IDE session starts within 3 seconds including container spin-up
Container sandbox cannot access host network (--network none enforced)
Resource limits enforced: OOM-killed containers return clear error to client
Code execution timeout kills container and returns timeout error after 30s
File system changes persist within session but are destroyed on session end
WebSocket reconnection restores session state within 5 seconds

plan.description: Implement the auto-grading engine: test runner orchestration, multi-language test harness adapters (pytest, Jest, cargo test), grade computation, partial credit scoring, and async result delivery via webhook.
acceptance_criteria: Grading job enqueued and completed within 60 seconds for standard submissions
Partial credit scoring computed correctly for rubric-based assignments
Grading results delivered via WebSocket push when client is connected; stored for async retrieval otherwise
Grading sandbox is isolated from IDE sandbox (separate container pool)
Grade history immutable after instructor sign-off (audit trail enforced)

plan.description: Design and implement the Code Review Bot using the review_and_critique agentic pattern. Generator agent analyzes student code for correctness, style, and best practices. Critic agent scores review quality and iterates until threshold. Delivers structured inline comments.

payload.output_format: GitHub-style inline comments with line references

acceptance_criteria: Code review generated within 45 seconds of trigger for files under 500 lines
Critic scores >= 0.85 before review delivered to student (verified by unit test)
Reviews include line-specific inline comments (not just summary)
Bot tone passes pedagogical tone audit: encouraging, specific, actionable
Review retrieves prior feedback from vector memory to avoid repetition for returning students
HITL gate available for instructors to approve/edit bot reviews before student sees them

plan.description: Build the project-based curriculum management system: curriculum CRUD with versioning, prerequisite graph, learning path generator, progress tracking, and content delivery (video, markdown, interactive exercises).
acceptance_criteria: Prerequisite graph enforces lesson ordering: student cannot access locked lesson
Curriculum versions tracked with rollback capability to any prior version
Progress data queryable per student per course for instructor dashboard
Content delivery supports Markdown (rendered), YouTube embeds, and interactive code exercises
Streak tracking updates daily; missed day breaks streak with correct calculation

plan.description: Implement job placement tracking: employer partner management, job listing ingestion, student profile/resume builder, application tracking, placement event recording (offer, acceptance, start date, salary), and analytics aggregation.

payload.privacy: FERPA — student placement data requires explicit consent to share with employers

acceptance_criteria: Employer partner onboarding API enforces signed employer_partner_agreement before data access
Student must explicitly consent (opt-in) before placement data shared with any employer
Placement rate analytics computable by cohort, course, and date range
Application tracker supports all 6 placement event states with timestamp logging
Salary data encrypted at rest; only anonymized aggregate stats exposed in public reports

plan.description: Build the core frontend shell: routing (React Router), global state management (Zustand), auth context, design system integration (Tailwind + component library), and skeleton layouts for all pages.
acceptance_criteria: All 12 routes render without errors in dev and prod builds
Auth context correctly gates protected routes; unauthenticated users redirected to login
WCAG 2.1 AA: axe-core scan returns 0 critical violations on all skeleton pages
Tailwind design tokens match Figma design system (colors, spacing, typography)
Lighthouse accessibility score >= 90 on student_dashboard

plan.description: Build the browser-based IDE component: Monaco Editor integration, file tree panel, integrated terminal (xterm.js), WebSocket connection to IDE backend, run/stop controls, and output panel with ANSI rendering.
acceptance_criteria: Editor loads within 2 seconds for files up to 1000 lines
Syntax highlighting works for Python, JavaScript, Rust, and Go
Terminal renders ANSI color codes correctly
WebSocket disconnection shows reconnecting indicator; auto-reconnects within 5 seconds
Run button disabled during active execution; stop button terminates sandbox container
WCAG 2.1 AA: editor keyboard-navigable; screen reader announces run/stop state changes

plan.description: Build lesson viewer, project submission UI, auto-grading results panel, and code review feedback interface with inline comment rendering.
acceptance_criteria: Markdown renders code blocks with syntax highlighting and copy button
Grading results panel updates in real-time via WebSocket without page reload
Inline code review comments anchor to correct line numbers even after file edits
Partial credit breakdown displays per-test scores with pass/fail indicators
Review history timeline shows all prior bot and instructor reviews chronologically
All interactive elements keyboard-accessible with visible focus indicators

plan.description: Build student dashboard, progress tracker, job board, placement tracker, and student profile/resume builder UI.
acceptance_criteria: Dashboard loads course progress and streak data within 1.5 seconds
Job board filterable by role, location, salary range, and employer
FERPA consent toggle clearly labeled; employer data sharing disabled by default
Resume builder generates downloadable PDF with student project portfolio
Placement timeline renders all 6 application states with timestamps
Mobile-responsive layout: all dashboard components usable on 375px viewport

plan.description: Build instructor dashboard and employer portal: course management, submission review queue, grade override, cohort analytics, job posting management, and candidate pipeline view.
acceptance_criteria: Instructor can create, publish, and unpublish a course without developer intervention
Submission queue shows real-time auto-grading status with instructor override capability
Grade override requires instructor confirmation dialog and creates audit log entry
Employer portal only shows candidates who have given explicit FERPA consent
Cohort analytics display completion rate, average grade, and placement rate by cohort
CodeReviewApproval UI allows instructors to edit bot reviews before student sees them

plan.description: Set up i18n infrastructure (react-i18next + i18next-backend), extract all UI strings to locale files, configure locale detection, and produce initial translations for English (base), Spanish, and Portuguese.
acceptance_criteria: Zero hardcoded UI strings in React components; all extracted to locale JSON files
Locale auto-detected from browser Accept-Language header with manual override in settings
Spanish and Portuguese translations cover 100% of navigation and error message keys
Legal notices (COPPA consent, FERPA disclosure) translated and reviewed by legal_advisor for each locale
RTL layout tested (Arabic placeholder): layout does not break
i18next missing key handler logs warning to console in dev; falls back to en-US

plan.description: Implement CI/CD pipelines (GitHub Actions): lint, test, build, Docker image push, Terraform plan/apply, Kubernetes rolling deploy, and smoke test gate. Set up monitoring (Prometheus + Grafana) and alerting (PagerDuty).
acceptance_criteria: CI pipeline completes in under 10 minutes for a standard PR
Prod deploy requires staging smoke tests to pass (enforced gate)
Grafana dashboards cover API latency, grading queue depth, IDE session count, and 5xx error rate
PagerDuty alert fires within 2 minutes when error rate > 1% for 5 consecutive minutes
Rolling deploy achieves zero-downtime for backend and frontend independently
Terraform plan runs on every PR targeting infra/ directory; apply requires manual approval

plan.description: Write comprehensive unit and integration tests for all backend modules: auth, grading engine, code review bot, curriculum graph, placement tracker, and IDE session manager.
acceptance_criteria: pytest --cov reports >= 85% line coverage across all backend modules
Integration tests use testcontainers (real DB) — no mock databases
Grading engine tests cover: correct output, wrong output, timeout, OOM, all 4 languages
Auth tests cover: valid login, expired token, wrong role, COPPA age-gate enforcement
Contract tests validate all API responses against openapi.yaml schema
All tests pass in CI within 5 minutes

plan.description: Write frontend unit tests (Vitest + React Testing Library) and E2E tests (Playwright) covering critical user flows: student onboarding, lesson completion, code submission, grading result display, job application, and FERPA consent.
acceptance_criteria: Vitest coverage >= 80% for all frontend components
All 6 critical E2E flows pass on Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit
E2E test for FERPA consent: job application blocked when consent toggle is off
E2E test for COPPA: registration redirects users who enter age < 13 to parental consent flow
Playwright tests run headless in CI; failures capture screenshot and trace

plan.description: Produce the master QA test plan, execute structured test cycles (functional, regression, accessibility, performance, security), and generate test execution reports for all platform modules.
acceptance_criteria: Test plan covers all 5 test types with entry/exit criteria defined
Functional test cases achieve >= 95% pass rate before release
Accessibility audit: 0 WCAG 2.1 AA critical violations across all pages
Manual screen reader test completed with NVDA (Windows) and VoiceOver (Mac) on student dashboard
Performance benchmarks: 95th percentile page load < 2s, IDE load < 3s measured with Playwright
Test execution report includes defect log with severity classification and resolution status

plan.description: Execute end-to-end system integration tests simulating full student and instructor lifecycle: enroll → complete lessons → submit project → receive grade → get code review → apply for job → placement recorded.
acceptance_criteria: Full student lifecycle scenario completes without error from registration to placement record
100 concurrent IDE sessions: P99 session start time < 5 seconds; zero sandbox escapes
50 simultaneous grading jobs: all complete within 90 seconds; no grade data cross-contamination
Code review bot quality sampling: critic score >= 0.85 verified on 20 sampled reviews
Load test: API sustains 500 RPS with P99 latency < 500ms and error rate < 0.1%
All system tests documented in report with pass/fail status and reproduction steps for failures

plan.description: Produce final compliance evidence package: COPPA age-gate test evidence, FERPA data access audit trail, WCAG 2.1 AA audit sign-off, and traceability matrix linking requirements to test results.
acceptance_criteria: Traceability matrix maps every COPPA/FERPA/WCAG requirement to at least one passing test
COPPA age-gate: screenshots and E2E test logs showing under-13 users blocked from direct registration
FERPA: audit log export showing every access to student educational records with user, timestamp, and purpose
WCAG 2.1 AA sign-off signed by ux_designer and qa_engineer
Privacy Impact Assessment countersigned by legal_advisor
Evidence package archived in immutable S3 bucket with access logging

plan.description: Produce user guides, onboarding video scripts, and training materials for students, instructors, and employer partners. Include IDE quick-start guide, curriculum authoring guide, and placement portal walkthrough.

payload.accessibility: Guides meet plain language standards (Flesch-Kincaid grade <= 10)

acceptance_criteria: Student getting started guide: new user can complete first lesson within 30 minutes following the guide alone
IDE quickstart covers run, stop, file creation, and terminal usage with annotated screenshots
Instructor authoring guide covers course creation, lesson types, project rubric setup, and grade override
Employer portal walkthrough covers job posting, candidate review, and FERPA consent verification
All guides reviewed by a non-technical user; comprehension issues resolved
Flesch-Kincaid grade level <= 10 for all user-facing guides (verified with readability tool)

plan.description: Write developer documentation: architecture overview, API reference (auto-generated from OpenAPI), local dev setup guide, contribution guide, database schema docs, and deployment runbook.
acceptance_criteria: New developer can clone repo and run `make dev` successfully following local dev setup guide alone
API reference auto-generated from openapi.yaml and published on every main branch merge
Architecture overview includes C4 context and container diagrams
Deployment runbook covers full prod deploy, rollback procedure, and post-deploy smoke test steps
ADR directory contains decision records for: IDE sandbox strategy, grading async model, FERPA consent UX
MkDocs site builds without warnings in CI

flaws: Docker-per-session IDE model will NOT achieve 3-second cold-start at any meaningful scale. Docker image pull + container init for Python/Rust/Go runtimes averages 4-12 seconds on cold nodes. Step 29 demands 100 concurrent sessions with P99 < 5s — at 512MB each that's 50GB RAM minimum, plus EKS node autoscaling adds 2-3 minutes latency. No pre-warming pool, no warm container strategy, no image layer caching policy is specified anywhere.

Rust cargo build under a 30-second execution timeout (Step 14) will fail for any non-trivial project. Rust cold-compile times routinely exceed 60-120 seconds for projects with dependencies. This makes the Rust language support effectively useless for project-based curriculum.

WebSocket horizontal scaling is unaddressed. FastAPI on EKS with multiple replicas cannot route WebSocket messages to the correct pod without sticky sessions (ALB cookie) or a Redis pub/sub fanout layer. When grading engine webhooks arrive (Step 15), the API has no mechanism to locate and push to the correct client WebSocket. This is a silent failure mode — grading results simply never reach students on multi-replica deployments.

Missing payment and enrollment billing system entirely. A coding bootcamp charges tuition — $5k-$15k+ in market. There is no Stripe integration, no payment plan support, no refund workflow, no revenue recognition, and no enrollment gate tied to payment status. This is not a nice-to-have; it is the business.

Cohort management is absent. Bootcamps operate in cohorts with fixed start dates, synchronized curriculum pacing, instructor assignment per cohort, and cohort-level graduation tracking. The schema (Step 8) has no cohort entity. The analytics in Step 18 reference 'placement_rate_by_cohort' without a mechanism to define or track cohorts.

Step 16 (Code Review Bot) specifies an agentic pattern with generator + critic agent but names zero LLM providers, no cost model, and no rate limiting. At scale — say, 500 students submitting daily — 3 critic iterations per review at GPT-4-class pricing exceeds $10k/month easily. There is no per-student quota, no cost circuit breaker, and no fallback when LLM is unavailable.

FERPA compliance is applied inconsistently. Steps 18 and 22 treat FERPA as scoped to placement data only. FERPA covers ALL educational records: grades (Step 15), code reviews (Step 16), submission history (Step 17), instructor notes — every record in the system. The RBAC model doesn't specify which instructor roles have 'legitimate educational interest' exceptions, which is the FERPA mechanism for intra-institutional access.

GDPR vs FERPA right-to-erasure conflict is unresolved. GDPR Article 17 requires data deletion on request. FERPA requires educational institutions to retain records for defined periods and restricts arbitrary deletion. Students in EU jurisdictions will trigger both. Step 6 acknowledges both regulations but no conflict resolution strategy or jurisdiction detection is specified.

Step 14 sandboxes specify '--network none enforced' but omit critical Linux security controls: --no-new-privileges, --cap-drop ALL, a custom seccomp profile, and AppArmor/SELinux policy. A determined student with 30 seconds of execution time and no capability restrictions has multiple kernel syscall attack surfaces. For a platform explicitly teaching systems programming (Rust, Go), this is a serious gap.

Step 17 'git-backed curriculum versions' is architecturally ambiguous. If this means storing actual git repos in the backend, it requires libgit2/pygit2, a separate git storage layer, and conflict resolution for concurrent instructor edits. If it means recording version snapshots in PostgreSQL, 'git-backed' is a misnomer. Either way, the implementation is underspecified and the dependency from Step 17 on Step 13 (basic auth/CRUD) is insufficient — curriculum versioning needs its own design step.

Monaco Editor IntelliSense (Step 20) requires language servers (LSP). For Python, this means running Pyright or Pylsp as a WebSocket-accessible backend service. For Rust, rust-analyzer. For Go, gopls. These are non-trivial backend services with their own resource profiles, not a frontend concern. None of these are in the infra plan, the backend steps, or the dependency graph. 'intellisense_basic' is not implementable without them.

Step 25 CI pipeline 'completes in under 10 minutes' is not achievable with the defined test suite. testcontainers spin-up alone adds 2-3 minutes. 85% backend coverage + contract tests + Playwright E2E on 3 browsers (Chromium, Firefox, WebKit) on 6 critical flows is realistically 25-45 minutes. Accepting a 10-minute constraint will force teams to skip tests or split CI arbitrarily.

Step 18 analytics (placement_rate, median_salary, time_to_hire) run as aggregation queries on the same PostgreSQL RDS instance serving OLTP traffic. No read replica, no materialized views, no ETL pipeline. At bootcamp scale these queries will table-scan millions of rows during peak enrollment periods and degrade API response times.

Step 14 accepts 'WebSocket reconnection restores session state within 5 seconds' but the session state lives in an ephemeral container. On reconnect, a NEW container must be started (another 3+ seconds), and the prior container's filesystem is destroyed per spec. There is no session state serialization or checkpoint mechanism defined. This acceptance criterion is contradictory to the filesystem destruction policy.
suggestions: Replace Docker-per-session with a pre-warmed container pool. Maintain N idle containers per language runtime, assign on session creation (sub-100ms), recycle on session end. Pool size tuned to concurrent user P95. This is how Replit and similar platforms achieve fast session starts.

Add a Redis-backed WebSocket session registry. Each WebSocket connection registers its pod IP + session ID in Redis on connect. When grading webhooks arrive, the API looks up the target pod and routes via internal HTTP or uses Redis pub/sub. This solves the multi-replica delivery problem completely.
Insert a Step 0 or pre-step for payment/billing: Stripe integration, enrollment flow, payment plan support, and subscription state tied to course access. This gates everything else.

Add a 'cohorts' entity to the database schema (Step 8) with start_date, end_date, instructor_assignment, and enrollment cap. Cascade cohort_id into submissions, grades, and placement records from the start.

Define an LLM cost budget and rate limiting policy in Step 16. Specify: max LLM calls per submission, daily per-student quota, monthly hard cap with alerting at 80%, and a fallback mode (linting-only review) when LLM is rate-limited or budget-exceeded.

Separate the 'FERPA legitimate educational interest' access model into a dedicated RBAC layer. Define exactly which roles can access which student records, log every access with purpose field, and enforce via middleware — not just application logic.

Add a GDPR-FERPA conflict resolution policy document to Step 6 legal artifacts. Minimally: jurisdiction detection by IP/signup country, different consent flows for EU vs US students, and a 'retention hold' mechanism that blocks erasure when FERPA retention applies.

Harden Step 14 sandboxes with explicit seccomp profile (deny all syscalls except approved list), --cap-drop ALL, --security-opt no-new-privileges, and consider gVisor (runsc) as the container runtime for defense-in-depth against kernel exploits.

Add language server services (Pyright, rust-analyzer, gopls) as dedicated backend microservices in the infra plan (Step 11) and API design (Step 9). These need resource limits, health checks, and connection pooling to the Monaco frontend.

Extend the Rust language timeout to at minimum 120 seconds for compilation, or separate 'build' from 'run' as distinct operations with independent timeouts. Document expected build times per language in the acceptance criteria.

Add a materialized view or nightly ETL job for placement analytics. Query the OLTP database for operational reports; query the materialized/analytical layer for aggregate dashboards. Add a read replica to the RDS configuration in Step 11.

Define session state serialization for IDE reconnection: checkpoint the container filesystem (tar snapshot to S3) on disconnect, restore on reconnect within a TTL window (e.g., 30 minutes). Amend the filesystem destruction policy to: 'destroyed after TTL or explicit session end, whichever comes first.'
missing: Payment and billing system: Stripe/payment processor integration, tuition collection, enrollment gate, refund workflow, revenue reconciliation
Cohort management entity and lifecycle: cohort creation, enrollment cap, pacing calendar, graduation criteria
Email notification system: grade notifications, job match alerts, instructor announcements, COPPA parental consent emails
Language Server Protocol (LSP) backend services for Monaco IntelliSense: Pyright (Python), rust-analyzer (Rust), gopls (Go), typescript-language-server (JS)
Secret management solution: AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault configuration, secret rotation policy, zero-secrets-in-env-vars enforcement
LLM provider specification and cost model for Code Review Bot (Step 16)
Container pre-warming pool design and idle container management strategy
Dead letter queue and back-pressure handling for grading queue (what happens when queue depth exceeds threshold)
Refresh token storage mechanism (HttpOnly cookie vs localStorage) and revocation strategy on logout
Row-level security (RLS) policy in PostgreSQL to prevent students from querying each other's submission rows directly
Incident response runbook: who gets paged, escalation path, rollback decision criteria
Data archival and retention policy: audit_logs and session_logs will grow unboundedly — no partitioning or archival schedule defined
Feature flag system for gradual rollout of code review bot and auto-grading to avoid all-or-nothing failures
GDPR-FERPA jurisdiction conflict resolution and right-to-erasure retention-hold mechanism
Dependabot or Renovate configuration for automated dependency CVE patching (SBOM is generated in Step 12 but no remediation automation exists)

Mobile strategy: browser IDE on 375px viewport (Step 22) is acknowledged as a constraint but no degraded mobile experience or native app strategy is defined for a platform where students may learn on phones
security_risks: Container sandbox insufficient hardening: --network none alone does not prevent privilege escalation. Missing: --cap-drop ALL, --no-new-privileges, custom seccomp profile denying dangerous syscalls (ptrace, mount, kexec_load). A student with Rust/Go execution access and 30 seconds can attempt kernel CVE exploitation. Consider gVisor (runsc) as the container runtime for the grading and IDE sandboxes.

JWT refresh token XSS exposure: Step 13 specifies 'refresh token rotation' but storage mechanism is unspecified. If stored in localStorage (common default), any XSS vulnerability (including via student-submitted code rendered in the UI) gives full session hijack. Mandate HttpOnly SameSite=Strict cookies for refresh tokens.

WebSocket authentication gap: initial WebSocket handshake can carry JWT in query param or header, but there is no per-message authentication or mid-session token refresh mechanism specified. A token that expires during an active IDE session will silently fail to push grading results.

Docker socket mount risk: if the container orchestrator (Step 14) runs inside a pod with Docker socket access, a sandbox escape gives root on the host. The architecture must specify that container orchestration uses Kubernetes Job API or a dedicated out-of-band orchestrator, never a mounted Docker socket.

Employer portal enumeration: employer API gives access to candidate profiles with explicit FERPA consent. No rate limiting per employer account is specified. A malicious employer could enumerate all consenting students via paginated API calls to harvest student data for off-platform recruitment.

Static analysis tools in Code Review Bot (Step 16) run against student code. If the static analysis tools themselves execute code (some do), the bot must run them in an isolated sandbox — not in the API process. This is unspecified and creates a code execution vector into the platform backend.

Curriculum version rollback (Step 17) with git-backed storage: if curriculum content is served directly from a git repo checkout, path traversal attacks via crafted lesson file paths could expose server filesystem contents. Requires strict input sanitization on all path parameters in content delivery.

COPPA age gate bypassability: the age gate is a UI/form-level control. Without backend-enforced age verification (requiring credit card proxy or parental email confirmation loop), any user can lie about their age. The compliance artifact (Step 30) needs to document the technical limitation and the accepted risk, or implement actual age verification.
final_review.summary: This is an unusually thorough plan for a complex platform — 32 well-sequenced steps, specific acceptance criteria, real compliance coverage (COPPA/FERPA/WCAG), and a sensible technology stack. The dependency graph is mostly correct and the phased MVP → v1 → v2 roadmap is realistic. However, three critical gaps will cause production failures regardless of how well the rest is implemented: (1) the Docker-per-session IDE model will miss every latency target at scale without a pre-warming pool strategy; (2) WebSocket delivery of grading results will silently fail in any multi-replica Kubernetes deployment without a Redis pub/sub routing layer; and (3) the entire business model lacks a payment and cohort management system. Beyond these blockers, the code review bot has no LLM cost model (a financial risk, not just a technical one), the Rust language support is functionally broken due to the 30-second timeout, and the sandbox security hardening is incomplete for a platform where students are explicitly taught systems programming. The compliance coverage is above average but FERPA is applied only to placement data when it covers all educational records. Score of 63 reflects: strong architecture and process thinking, but three unacceptable gaps for an MVP that claims to handle student records and live code execution.

flaws: Docker-per-session IDE model will NOT achieve 3-second cold-start at any meaningful scale. Docker image pull + container init for Python/Rust/Go runtimes averages 4-12 seconds on cold nodes. Step 29 demands 100 concurrent sessions with P99 < 5s — at 512MB each that's 50GB RAM minimum, plus EKS node autoscaling adds 2-3 minutes latency. No pre-warming pool, no warm container strategy, no image layer caching policy is specified anywhere.

Rust cargo build under a 30-second execution timeout (Step 14) will fail for any non-trivial project. Rust cold-compile times routinely exceed 60-120 seconds for projects with dependencies. This makes the Rust language support effectively useless for project-based curriculum.

WebSocket horizontal scaling is unaddressed. FastAPI on EKS with multiple replicas cannot route WebSocket messages to the correct pod without sticky sessions (ALB cookie) or a Redis pub/sub fanout layer. When grading engine webhooks arrive (Step 15), the API has no mechanism to locate and push to the correct client WebSocket. This is a silent failure mode — grading results simply never reach students on multi-replica deployments.

Missing payment and enrollment billing system entirely. A coding bootcamp charges tuition — $5k-$15k+ in market. There is no Stripe integration, no payment plan support, no refund workflow, no revenue recognition, and no enrollment gate tied to payment status. This is not a nice-to-have; it is the business.

Cohort management is absent. Bootcamps operate in cohorts with fixed start dates, synchronized curriculum pacing, instructor assignment per cohort, and cohort-level graduation tracking. The schema (Step 8) has no cohort entity. The analytics in Step 18 reference 'placement_rate_by_cohort' without a mechanism to define or track cohorts.

Step 16 (Code Review Bot) specifies an agentic pattern with generator + critic agent but names zero LLM providers, no cost model, and no rate limiting. At scale — say, 500 students submitting daily — 3 critic iterations per review at GPT-4-class pricing exceeds $10k/month easily. There is no per-student quota, no cost circuit breaker, and no fallback when LLM is unavailable.

FERPA compliance is applied inconsistently. Steps 18 and 22 treat FERPA as scoped to placement data only. FERPA covers ALL educational records: grades (Step 15), code reviews (Step 16), submission history (Step 17), instructor notes — every record in the system. The RBAC model doesn't specify which instructor roles have 'legitimate educational interest' exceptions, which is the FERPA mechanism for intra-institutional access.

GDPR vs FERPA right-to-erasure conflict is unresolved. GDPR Article 17 requires data deletion on request. FERPA requires educational institutions to retain records for defined periods and restricts arbitrary deletion. Students in EU jurisdictions will trigger both. Step 6 acknowledges both regulations but no conflict resolution strategy or jurisdiction detection is specified.

Step 14 sandboxes specify '--network none enforced' but omit critical Linux security controls: --no-new-privileges, --cap-drop ALL, a custom seccomp profile, and AppArmor/SELinux policy. A determined student with 30 seconds of execution time and no capability restrictions has multiple kernel syscall attack surfaces. For a platform explicitly teaching systems programming (Rust, Go), this is a serious gap.

Step 17 'git-backed curriculum versions' is architecturally ambiguous. If this means storing actual git repos in the backend, it requires libgit2/pygit2, a separate git storage layer, and conflict resolution for concurrent instructor edits. If it means recording version snapshots in PostgreSQL, 'git-backed' is a misnomer. Either way, the implementation is underspecified and the dependency from Step 17 on Step 13 (basic auth/CRUD) is insufficient — curriculum versioning needs its own design step.

Monaco Editor IntelliSense (Step 20) requires language servers (LSP). For Python, this means running Pyright or Pylsp as a WebSocket-accessible backend service. For Rust, rust-analyzer. For Go, gopls. These are non-trivial backend services with their own resource profiles, not a frontend concern. None of these are in the infra plan, the backend steps, or the dependency graph. 'intellisense_basic' is not implementable without them.

Step 25 CI pipeline 'completes in under 10 minutes' is not achievable with the defined test suite. testcontainers spin-up alone adds 2-3 minutes. 85% backend coverage + contract tests + Playwright E2E on 3 browsers (Chromium, Firefox, WebKit) on 6 critical flows is realistically 25-45 minutes. Accepting a 10-minute constraint will force teams to skip tests or split CI arbitrarily.

Step 18 analytics (placement_rate, median_salary, time_to_hire) run as aggregation queries on the same PostgreSQL RDS instance serving OLTP traffic. No read replica, no materialized views, no ETL pipeline. At bootcamp scale these queries will table-scan millions of rows during peak enrollment periods and degrade API response times.

Step 14 accepts 'WebSocket reconnection restores session state within 5 seconds' but the session state lives in an ephemeral container. On reconnect, a NEW container must be started (another 3+ seconds), and the prior container's filesystem is destroyed per spec. There is no session state serialization or checkpoint mechanism defined. This acceptance criterion is contradictory to the filesystem destruction policy.
suggestions: Replace Docker-per-session with a pre-warmed container pool. Maintain N idle containers per language runtime, assign on session creation (sub-100ms), recycle on session end. Pool size tuned to concurrent user P95. This is how Replit and similar platforms achieve fast session starts.

Add a Redis-backed WebSocket session registry. Each WebSocket connection registers its pod IP + session ID in Redis on connect. When grading webhooks arrive, the API looks up the target pod and routes via internal HTTP or uses Redis pub/sub. This solves the multi-replica delivery problem completely.
Insert a Step 0 or pre-step for payment/billing: Stripe integration, enrollment flow, payment plan support, and subscription state tied to course access. This gates everything else.

Add a 'cohorts' entity to the database schema (Step 8) with start_date, end_date, instructor_assignment, and enrollment cap. Cascade cohort_id into submissions, grades, and placement records from the start.

Define an LLM cost budget and rate limiting policy in Step 16. Specify: max LLM calls per submission, daily per-student quota, monthly hard cap with alerting at 80%, and a fallback mode (linting-only review) when LLM is rate-limited or budget-exceeded.

Separate the 'FERPA legitimate educational interest' access model into a dedicated RBAC layer. Define exactly which roles can access which student records, log every access with purpose field, and enforce via middleware — not just application logic.

Add a GDPR-FERPA conflict resolution policy document to Step 6 legal artifacts. Minimally: jurisdiction detection by IP/signup country, different consent flows for EU vs US students, and a 'retention hold' mechanism that blocks erasure when FERPA retention applies.

Harden Step 14 sandboxes with explicit seccomp profile (deny all syscalls except approved list), --cap-drop ALL, --security-opt no-new-privileges, and consider gVisor (runsc) as the container runtime for defense-in-depth against kernel exploits.

Add language server services (Pyright, rust-analyzer, gopls) as dedicated backend microservices in the infra plan (Step 11) and API design (Step 9). These need resource limits, health checks, and connection pooling to the Monaco frontend.

Extend the Rust language timeout to at minimum 120 seconds for compilation, or separate 'build' from 'run' as distinct operations with independent timeouts. Document expected build times per language in the acceptance criteria.

Add a materialized view or nightly ETL job for placement analytics. Query the OLTP database for operational reports; query the materialized/analytical layer for aggregate dashboards. Add a read replica to the RDS configuration in Step 11.

Define session state serialization for IDE reconnection: checkpoint the container filesystem (tar snapshot to S3) on disconnect, restore on reconnect within a TTL window (e.g., 30 minutes). Amend the filesystem destruction policy to: 'destroyed after TTL or explicit session end, whichever comes first.'
missing: Payment and billing system: Stripe/payment processor integration, tuition collection, enrollment gate, refund workflow, revenue reconciliation
Cohort management entity and lifecycle: cohort creation, enrollment cap, pacing calendar, graduation criteria
Email notification system: grade notifications, job match alerts, instructor announcements, COPPA parental consent emails
Language Server Protocol (LSP) backend services for Monaco IntelliSense: Pyright (Python), rust-analyzer (Rust), gopls (Go), typescript-language-server (JS)
Secret management solution: AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault configuration, secret rotation policy, zero-secrets-in-env-vars enforcement
LLM provider specification and cost model for Code Review Bot (Step 16)
Container pre-warming pool design and idle container management strategy
Dead letter queue and back-pressure handling for grading queue (what happens when queue depth exceeds threshold)
Refresh token storage mechanism (HttpOnly cookie vs localStorage) and revocation strategy on logout
Row-level security (RLS) policy in PostgreSQL to prevent students from querying each other's submission rows directly
Incident response runbook: who gets paged, escalation path, rollback decision criteria
Data archival and retention policy: audit_logs and session_logs will grow unboundedly — no partitioning or archival schedule defined
Feature flag system for gradual rollout of code review bot and auto-grading to avoid all-or-nothing failures
GDPR-FERPA jurisdiction conflict resolution and right-to-erasure retention-hold mechanism
Dependabot or Renovate configuration for automated dependency CVE patching (SBOM is generated in Step 12 but no remediation automation exists)

Mobile strategy: browser IDE on 375px viewport (Step 22) is acknowledged as a constraint but no degraded mobile experience or native app strategy is defined for a platform where students may learn on phones
security_risks: Container sandbox insufficient hardening: --network none alone does not prevent privilege escalation. Missing: --cap-drop ALL, --no-new-privileges, custom seccomp profile denying dangerous syscalls (ptrace, mount, kexec_load). A student with Rust/Go execution access and 30 seconds can attempt kernel CVE exploitation. Consider gVisor (runsc) as the container runtime for the grading and IDE sandboxes.

JWT refresh token XSS exposure: Step 13 specifies 'refresh token rotation' but storage mechanism is unspecified. If stored in localStorage (common default), any XSS vulnerability (including via student-submitted code rendered in the UI) gives full session hijack. Mandate HttpOnly SameSite=Strict cookies for refresh tokens.

WebSocket authentication gap: initial WebSocket handshake can carry JWT in query param or header, but there is no per-message authentication or mid-session token refresh mechanism specified. A token that expires during an active IDE session will silently fail to push grading results.

Docker socket mount risk: if the container orchestrator (Step 14) runs inside a pod with Docker socket access, a sandbox escape gives root on the host. The architecture must specify that container orchestration uses Kubernetes Job API or a dedicated out-of-band orchestrator, never a mounted Docker socket.

Employer portal enumeration: employer API gives access to candidate profiles with explicit FERPA consent. No rate limiting per employer account is specified. A malicious employer could enumerate all consenting students via paginated API calls to harvest student data for off-platform recruitment.

Static analysis tools in Code Review Bot (Step 16) run against student code. If the static analysis tools themselves execute code (some do), the bot must run them in an isolated sandbox — not in the API process. This is unspecified and creates a code execution vector into the platform backend.

Curriculum version rollback (Step 17) with git-backed storage: if curriculum content is served directly from a git repo checkout, path traversal attacks via crafted lesson file paths could expose server filesystem contents. Requires strict input sanitization on all path parameters in content delivery.

COPPA age gate bypassability: the age gate is a UI/form-level control. Without backend-enforced age verification (requiring credit card proxy or parental email confirmation loop), any user can lie about their age. The compliance artifact (Step 30) needs to document the technical limitation and the accepted risk, or implement actual age verification.
history.summary: This is an unusually thorough plan for a complex platform — 32 well-sequenced steps, specific acceptance criteria, real compliance coverage (COPPA/FERPA/WCAG), and a sensible technology stack. The dependency graph is mostly correct and the phased MVP → v1 → v2 roadmap is realistic. However, three critical gaps will cause production failures regardless of how well the rest is implemented: (1) the Docker-per-session IDE model will miss every latency target at scale without a pre-warming pool strategy; (2) WebSocket delivery of grading results will silently fail in any multi-replica Kubernetes deployment without a Redis pub/sub routing layer; and (3) the entire business model lacks a payment and cohort management system. Beyond these blockers, the code review bot has no LLM cost model (a financial risk, not just a technical one), the Rust language support is functionally broken due to the 30-second timeout, and the sandbox security hardening is incomplete for a platform where students are explicitly taught systems programming. The compliance coverage is above average but FERPA is applied only to placement data when it covers all educational records. Score of 63 reflects: strong architecture and process thinking, but three unacceptable gaps for an MVP that claims to handle student records and live code execution.
